title: Advanced OOP Quiz 🏆
description: Prove your architectural skills! Master Inheritance, Overrides, Abstracts, and Mixins.

questions.question: What keyword is used to make a child class inherit from a parent class?
questions.explanation: In Dart, `extends` establishes the parent-child inheritance relationship.

questions.question: If `class Car extends Vehicle{}`, what is the relationship?
options: Car is the parent, Vehicle is the child.
Car is the child, Vehicle is the parent.
questions.explanation: The child class always sits on the left side of the `extends` keyword.

questions.question: A child class inherits all properties and methods from its parent class.
questions.explanation: Yes, everything public in the parent becomes freely accessible within the child.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of writing `@override` above a method in a child class?
options: To explicitly tell the compiler (and other humans) that you are replacing the parent's generic logic with custom logic.
To make the method run twice as fast.
questions.explanation: Overriding allows subclasses to adapt inherited methods to suit their specific needs.

questions.question: If you override a method, but you still want to run the parent's original code inside you, you call _______.methodName();
questions.explanation: The 'super' keyword specifically points to the parent/superclass logic.

questions.question: Can you use the `extends` keyword multiple times on a single class (e.g., `class Dog extends Animal extends Pet`)?
options: No, Dart strictly enforces Single Inheritance. A class can only have ONE direct parent.
questions.explanation: Dart classes can only `extend` a single parent class to avoid complex hierarchy collisions.

questions.question: What does declaring a class as `abstract` do?
options: It prevents you from ever creating a direct Object from that class, forcing it to be used purely as a template.
questions.explanation: Abstract classes are foundational templates. They cannot be instantiated directly using `()`.

questions.question: An abstract class can contain methods that have absolutely no code inside them (no `{}` body).
questions.explanation: Yes! These are called abstract methods. `void calculateArea();` is totally valid inside an abstract class.

questions.question: If an abstract parent class has an empty method `void draw();`... what MUST the child class do?
options: The child class must ignore it.
The child class MUST write an @override for `draw()` and provide actual code for it.
The child class will crash immediately.
questions.explanation: The empty method acts as a strict contract. The child is heavily penalized by the compiler if it fails to provide the implementation.

questions.question: To solve the Single Inheritance limit, Dart allows you to inject reusable chunks of code from any family tree. This chunk is called a ______.
questions.explanation: Mixins are dedicated blocks of 'superpowers' designed specifically for cross-family injection.

questions.question: What keyword brings a Mixin into your Class?
questions.explanation: You attach mixins using the `with` keyword. e.g. `class Hero with Flyer`

questions.question: You can attach 5 different mixins to a single class, separating them with commas.
questions.explanation: Absolutely! `class Hero extends Human with Flyer, Swimmer, Fighter` is perfectly legal dart code.

questions.question: When building an iOS / Android app in Flutter, you will primarily interact with objects by:
options: Building classes that `extend` Flutter's pre-built interface widgets.
questions.explanation: Everything in Flutter is an object. You will constantly write `class MyScreen extends Widget`.

questions.question: Instead of copy-pasting code across twenty classes, the core DRY principle says you should use _______ to share the common traits.
questions.explanation: Inheritance (along with mixins) is the foundational tool for preventing duplicate code in OOP.

questions.question: If `Dog` extends `Animal`, and `GoldenRetriever` extends `Dog`... does the GoldenRetriever inherit properties from `Animal`?
options: Yes, inheritance flows all the way down the family tree.
No, it only inherits directly from Dog.
questions.explanation: Inheritance chains downward indefinitely. The Retriever gets everything from Dog, and everything Dog got from Animal.

questions.question: If you override a method but FORGET to put `@override` at the top, what happens?
options: The code still technically works, but the compiler warns you because it's confusing for humans to read without the tag.
questions.explanation: While it technically compiles, omitting `@override` is very dangerous because you lose the compiler's safety nets if the parent method name ever changes.

questions.question: An abstract class can only contain empty methods. It cannot contain regular methods with actual code.
questions.explanation: False! Abstract classes are extremely flexible. They can contain a mix of both rigid empty methods (contracts) AND full methods with actual code ready to be inherited.

questions.question: Which represents a Mixin injection?
questions.explanation: The `with` keyword designates the injection of a Mixin 'B' into Class 'A'.

questions.question: A mixin is defined using the keyword: ______ Flyer { void fly() {} }
questions.explanation: You write `mixin Name {}` to declare the superpower.

questions.question: If `Animal` has `void sleep()` and you write `class Fish extends Animal` but DO NOT override `sleep()`, what happens?
options: The Fish class silently uses the exact `sleep()` logic defined in Animal.
questions.explanation: Unless the parent method was completely empty (abstract), the child automatically gets the parent's default implementation.
